label: Optimization and search heuristics
description: Methods for searching large solution spaces when exact optimization is computationally infeasible.
bridging_explanation: These heuristic techniques aren't directly covered in the MBAN toolkit, but the optimization and decision-analysis concepts you learned provide the academic foundation. LP and IP give exact optimal solutions for linear problems; when problems are too complex for exact methods (NP-hard, combinatorial), heuristics like genetic algorithms approximate solutions using the same objective-function logic you know from formulation.

label: Dimensionality reduction
description: Methods for reducing high-dimensional feature spaces to fewer dimensions while preserving structure.
bridging_explanation: PCA and t-SNE are dimensionality reduction techniques not directly covered, but the curse of dimensionality you studied in kNN directly motivates them: as features increase, distance metrics break down and models overfit. Random forest feature importance (which you used) is a form of supervised feature selection. PCA is the unsupervised counterpart — it finds the axes of maximum variance in the data.

label: Causal inference
description: Methods for identifying causal effects rather than correlations — moving from association to causation.
bridging_explanation: Causal inference is a critical extension beyond what MBAN 5520 teaches. In regression you learned how to estimate associations and interpret coefficients — causal inference asks when those coefficients represent true causal effects. RCTs (randomization) are the gold standard; instrumental variables and DiD are econometric approaches when randomization isn't possible. The prediction-vs-causation distinction is central to MBAN 5510's evaluation of AI outputs.

label: Advanced time series methods
description: Statistical and ML methods specifically designed for temporal data with autocorrelation and seasonality.
bridging_explanation: ARIMA and Prophet are classical time series methods not explicitly covered in the MBAN toolkit. However, you studied LSTM and sequential forecasting (Wk 9-10 of MBAN 5560) which are the deep learning counterpart to ARIMA — both model temporal dependencies, but LSTMs learn them from data rather than specifying the AR/MA order. Understanding sequential structure from LSTM gives you the intuition to understand ARIMA's autoregressive and moving-average components.

label: Reinforcement learning
description: Learning through reward signals rather than labeled data — agents take actions to maximize cumulative reward.
bridging_explanation: Reinforcement learning is not directly covered in the MBAN toolkit, but the agentic AI systems from MBAN 5510 share key ideas: both involve an agent taking actions, observing outcomes, and updating behavior. The ReAct loop (reason-act-observe) mirrors the RL perception-action cycle. Markov chains and dynamic programming from MBAN 5540 are the mathematical foundations of Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) that underpin Q-learning and policy gradient methods.

label: Recommendation systems
description: Systems that suggest relevant items to users based on behavior patterns and item similarities.
bridging_explanation: Recommendation systems use similarity concepts you learned in kNN (distance-based neighbors) and clustering (grouping similar users/items). Collaborative filtering is essentially kNN applied to user-item rating matrices. Content-based filtering uses feature similarity — the same cosine similarity logic as word2vec/NLP embeddings you covered in MBAN 5560 (Wk 10-11). Matrix factorization (the core of Netflix-style recommenders) is a linear algebra decomposition related to the dimensionality concepts in your toolkit.

label: Graph and network methods
description: Methods that represent data as nodes and edges — social networks, knowledge graphs, molecular graphs.
bridging_explanation: Graph neural networks and knowledge graphs are not directly covered in the MBAN toolkit, but you built stateful directed graphs in LangGraph (MBAN 5510) — nodes represent functions and edges represent control flow. This is the same graph abstraction used in GNNs and knowledge graphs. Community detection in social networks uses the same clustering intuition from K-means and DBSCAN. PageRank (the algorithm that made Google) uses iterative graph traversal similar to Markov chains from MBAN 5540.

label: Bayesian methods
description: Probabilistic methods that update beliefs based on evidence using Bayes' theorem.
bridging_explanation: MCMC and Bayesian networks are extensions you haven't covered directly, but they build directly on what you've studied. Monte Carlo simulation (MBAN 5570 with @RISK) is a frequentist cousin of MCMC — both sample repeatedly to estimate distributions. Bayesian sampling in decision analysis (MBAN 5540) is the prior-to-posterior update you studied for EVPI calculations. Bayesian networks generalize decision trees by allowing probabilistic dependencies between variables.

label: Advanced computer vision
description: Deep learning methods for image understanding beyond basic classification.
bridging_explanation: Object detection and image segmentation are advanced computer vision tasks that build directly on the CNNs you covered in MBAN 5560 (Wk 9). YOLO and R-CNN add spatial localization heads on top of a CNN feature extractor — the same convolutional architecture you learned. GANs and diffusion models (used for image generation) use the same gradient-based training, but with an adversarial or denoising objective rather than cross-entropy classification.

label: Advanced NLP methods
description: NLP techniques for structured information extraction, summarization, and language generation.
bridging_explanation: Named entity recognition, text summarization, and RAG are advanced NLP applications that sit on top of the transformer architecture you studied in MBAN 5560 (Wk 10-11). BERT-based NER uses the same fine-tuning pipeline you learned with Hugging Face. RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) extends LangChain agents with vector database retrieval — the same LangChain abstractions from MBAN 5510. Financial NLP (MBAN 5570) applied sentiment analysis to earnings calls, which is the same sequence classification task.
